claim: The Heath brothers' SUCCESs framework prescribes Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, Stories — strip ideas to a one-sentence core, violate expectations, use sensory detail, let the audience verify (Reagan's 'are you better off than four years ago?'), trigger emotion, and use narrative as mental rehearsal.
quote: If you argue ten points, even if each is a good point, when they get back to the jury room they won't remember any... Mentally rehearsing a situation helps us perform better when we encounter that situation in the physical environment.

claim: Concreteness is the antidote to abstraction: tangible, sensory ideas (an ice-filled bathtub, a tube in the back) are far more memorable than corporate abstractions like 'synergies'.
quote: Concrete ideas are far more memorable than abstract ones. To make your idea stick, it must be tangible.

claim: TED curator Chris Anderson caps talks at 18 minutes because it is 'short enough to hold people's attention' and forces speakers to discipline their thinking; researchers in the early 1980s identified 'cognitive backlog' where piling on information makes audiences drop everything.
quote: 18 minutes long is the perfect length of time... short enough to hold people's attention, including on the Internet, and precise enough to be taken seriously... cognitive backlog — the more you pile on, the more likely you are to drop everything.

claim: Molecular biologist John Medina's 10-Minute Rule: peer-reviewed studies show audiences mentally check out around the 10-minute mark, so lectures must be chunked into ~10-minute segments each ending with an emotionally relevant hook (anecdote/story) to reset attention — the working model for any 60-min talk.
quote: Before the first quarter-hour is over in a typical presentation, people usually have checked out... I divide my lectures into 10-minute segments. Each segment covers a single core concept... I always finish a segment by giving (the brain) a break... an emotionally relevant example.

claim: Practical application of Medina's rule: structure a long talk as 10-minute chunks, each opening with the core idea, supporting it with detail, and closing with a 'hook' — a story, demo, or surprise — before moving on; this is how Medina sustains 50-minute lectures.
quote: Medina divides his lectures into discrete 10-minute modules... He concludes each module with a 'hook' — a relevant story, anecdote, or even joke — that buys him another 10 minutes of attention.

claim: Hans Rosling's method for non-experts: animate the data so the eye is led, narrate what it means in plain language ('down here is poor and sick, up here is rich and healthy'), inject genuine emotion ('what a catastrophe!'), and challenge a worldview the audience already holds.
quote: Hans is a master at telling the story behind the numbers... He challenges our assumptions and changes our perceptions about the world we live in.

claim: Rosling's lessons for technical-to-lay translation: establish context before data ('the why'), use motion/animation to reduce cognitive load, and treat statistics as characters in a narrative arc rather than evidence dumped on slides.
quote: Rosling established context before showing the information, and most importantly answered the question of 'why?'... An introduction reduces cognitive load on your audience.

claim: Bruce Schneier's accessible-security style relies heavily on analogies (Yogi Berra, Star Wars, Greek mythology) and refuses to be either overly technical or sensational — the two failure modes of cybersecurity writing aimed at the public.
quote: Schneier peppers his writing with Yogi Berra quotes, analogies drawn from 'Star Wars' and trivia tidbits from Greek mythology to aim straight for the general audience... his use of analogies makes his work very readable.

claim: Schneier's core reframe for non-technical audiences: security is a people problem, not a technology problem — 'if you think technology can solve your security problems, then you don't understand the problems and you don't understand technology.'
quote: If you think technology can solve your security problems, then you don't understand the problems and you don't understand technology.

claim: For lay security audiences the most reliably understood analogy families are home/house (locks, alarms, neighborhood watch = access control, IDS, threat-intel), healthcare (treatment plans = tailored security programs), and transportation rules — domains the audience already has rich mental models for.
quote: Housing is a fantastic way to describe access controls, at-rest encryption, on-site vs. off-site decisions... shared threat intelligence, managed incident response, firewalls, and intrusion detection... neighborhood watch, home alarm response, fencing and security gates.

claim: Caveat from a former Goldman Sachs CISO: security analogies become counterproductive when stretched past their useful range — they let executives feel they understand without engaging the actual mechanism, so use them as on-ramps, not as the whole explanation.
quote: Analogies are useful for initial framing but can be misleading or counterproductive when overused or extended beyond their valid range.

claim: Peer-reviewed guidance on tailoring scientific communication: jargon that functions as efficient shorthand inside a discipline alienates non-experts, and 'nonexpert audiences may respond better to a simplified version of research that focuses clearly on significance and impact' rather than method.
quote: Technical terms and jargon function as short-hand within the field, but these same words will alienate the general public... Nonexpert audiences may respond better to a simplified version of research that focuses clearly on significance and impact.

claim: Stanford GSB's Jennifer Aaker is the source most often cited for 'stories are remembered up to 22x more than facts alone'; the underlying point — narrative dramatically outperforms bare statistics for retention — holds even where the exact 22x figure is contested (other studies show ~12-13x).
quote: Research shows that our brains are not hard-wired to understand logic or retain facts for very long. Our brains are wired to understand and retain stories.

claim: WEF (Oct 2025) framing for non-technical AI audiences: AI literacy is judgment about when to trust output, not technical depth — frame risks (deepfakes, hallucination, bias) around concrete decisions a layperson actually makes rather than model architecture.
quote: AI literacy is not about sounding technical — it is about developing enough understanding and judgment to use AI without becoming careless, overconfident, or dependent on it for the wrong things.

claim: The 2026 International AI Safety Report's policymaker summary models the 'explain to non-experts' register: lead with concrete harms (misinformation, bias, opacity), avoid model-internals jargon, and frame uncertainty explicitly so the audience knows what is and isn't known.
quote: Developers still understand little about how their general-purpose AI models operate, making it more difficult both to predict behavioural issues and to explain and resolve known issues.
